figures.caption: FIG. 4: (a) A 2-D t-SNE visualization of task vectors for the K = 64 model demonstrates increased separability through training. (b) Results of task vector patching experiment. Replacing the task vector formed on a sequence from transition matrix A with that formed on a sequence from transition matrix B results in the transformer predicting according to the transition matrix B. See Appendix IX 3 for details. (c) The information content of the task vectors is optimized through training. Mutual information is presented as a fraction of the channel capacity log2K. See Appendix IX 4 for details.

FIG. 8: (a) The time between the onset of G2 and its disappearance in favor of M2 diverges as (K∗ 2 −K)−γ with γ ≈ 2 and K∗ 2 ≈ 7000. The model is assumed to enter M2 when it achieves a training loss Ltrain < L2-Gen. Median value over 4 different seeds trained on the same task distribution DT shown, with errorbars indicating the 95% confidence interval. Dashed line indicates a divergence fit to the medians with a linear offset. Inset: Replot of the data highlighting asymptotic behavior. The fit value for K∗ 2 was used to compute the offset K∗ 2 −K. Dashed line with slope −γ provided for reference. (b) The mean fraction of memorized tasks extrapolated to convergence decreases linearly past K ≈ 28. See Appendix XI for details. A linear extrapolation of these data suggests that the model will not memorize any chain for K ≈ 5000, which is consistent with the estimate for K∗ 2 from the data in panel (a). Errorbars show standard deviation over the 4 seeds. (c) Schematic of the minimal network inspired from the circuit-tracing results in Figure 3, used to reproduce the 2-Mem behavior shown in the remaining panels. (d,e) Heat maps of Ltrain − L2-Gen and Lgen − L2-Gen as functions of the task-vector embedding dimension Dφ and data diversity K. Blue (red) indicates that the model is over (under) performing relative to the 2-Gen predictor; white denotes the crossover. (f) Average critical data diversity K̂∗ 2 over three independent trials. The labels (ℓ1, ℓ2) indicate the number of hidden layers in MLP1 and MLP2, respectively. The standard deviation error bar is estimated with three independent trials.

TABLE I: Explicit definition of source vectors contributing to the residual stream, and the target perturbations. To ablate the edge from source SRC to a target TGT at layer ℓ, we compute the perturbed input as

FIG. 7: (a) The maximum value of the induction head parameter ϕ(2)β through training is sigmoidal as the data diversity crosses over K∗ 1 . This indicates that the model either reaches the threshold and proceeds to G2 (high ϕ(2)β ) or does not reach the threshold and proceeds to M1, with few intermediates. Evaluated over 16 different model seeds trained on the same task distribution. Each seed has been shifted along the horizontal axis such that ϕ(2)β first crosses the threshold value ϕ∗β at K = K∗ 1 ). See Appendix X for details. (b) Bimodality of the model dynamics near K∗ 1 , shown for K = 94 ≈ K∗ 1 and 64 seeds trained on the same DT . Different model seeds show either small τ2-Gen or never achieve G2. All model seeds in the rightmost bin did not enter G2 after 5× 104 iterations. (c) Perturbations to the learning dynamics of the competing sub-circuits have effects on the memorization-generalization transition that are explainable by a kinetic competition. K∗ 1 increases when the effective learning rate of the 2-Gen circuit γ2-Gen is reduced (LR perturbation). Increasing the rate of memorization by providing information about the ground truth task identity also increases K∗ 1 (TI perturbation). Kernel density estimated from 4 seeds.

FIG. 2: (a) Training dynamics of the transformer training loss (top) and generalization loss (bottom) at low, medium, and high K. Notice the plateaus during training that occur near the predictor training and out-of-distribution losses. The model dynamics and predictor losses have been produced by evaluating model checkpoints and the predictors on a large batch of sequences. We have omitted for clarity the predictor losses near which each model does not exhibit plateaus. (b) Behavioral readouts of the transformer through training across a range of K. Color intensity is inversely proportional to the divergence (in nats) of the transformer predictions from the indicated predictor (Appendix IV 1). Simultaneous similarity of the model to both 1-Gen and 1-Mem predictors at large K results from the relatively high similarity of these predictors to the uniform predictor over states. (c) Mechanistic readouts and order parameters of the transformer through training across a range of K (Appendix IV 2 for details). Here, n(1)A and n(2) A have been normalized by the sequence length, N = 256. The correspondence between phases and mechanistic readouts are as follows: G1 and M1: high n(1) A and low ϕ (1) δ ; G2: high ϕ(1)δ and ϕ(2)β ; M2: high ϕ(1)δ and n(2) A .
